---
title: "Benchmarking Fisheries Integrated Modeling System: A Next-Generation Framework for Reliable Fisheries Stock Assessments" 
author:
  - name: Bai Li
    email: bai.li@noaa.gov
    affiliations: 
        - id: ecs
          name: ECS Federal LLC in support of NOAA Fisheries Office of Science and Technology
          address: 1315 East-West Highway, Building SSMC3
          city: Silver Spring
          state: MD
          postal-code: 20910
    attributes:
        corresponding: true
  - name: Bob Security
    email: bob@example.com
    affiliations:
        - id: another-u
          name: Another University
          department: Department Name
          address: Street Address
          city: City
          state: State
          postal-code: Postal Code
  - name: Cat Memes
    email: cat@example.com
    affiliations:
        - ref: another-u
abstract: |
  As NOAA Fisheries transitions to next-generation stock assessment methods, including the Fisheries Integrated Modeling System (FIMS), it is important to evaluate how its performance compares with established modeling tools. Using an age-structured operating model, we compared FIMS estimates with four widely used assessment models in the United States. Comparisons with ADMB-based models, the Age Structured Assessment Program, Stock Synthesis, and the Beaufort Assessment Model, focused on verifying estimates of key assessment quantities, including spawning biomass, recruitment, and fishing mortality. Comparisons with the Woods Hole Assessment Model focused on verifying estimates of random effects. Results indicate that estimates from FIMS are highly consistent with those from established modeling frameworks, with median relative error closely aligned across key metrics. Differences among models were generally minor and primarily associated with differences in the sum-to-zero constraint on recruitment deviations, equilibrium initialization for initial numbers-at-age, and recruitment bias correction. Overall, these findings demonstrate that FIMS provides a reliable and modular framework for U.S. fisheries stock assessment while maintaining high standards of estimation accuracy and enabling flexible random-effects modeling.
keywords: 
  - Model Comparison
  - Fisheries Integrated Modeling System
  - Random Effects
date: last-modified
bibliography: bibliography.bib
format:
  elsevier-pdf:
    keep-tex: true
    journal:
      name: TBD
      formatting: preprint
      # model: 3p # Don't set a model with preprint
      cite-style: authoryear
---

# Introduction

- NOAA Fisheries is developing FIMS as a next-generation, extensible stock assessment framework.
- Previous studies showed Age Structured Assessment Program (ASAP), Beaufort Assessment Model (BAM), and Stock Synthesis (SS3) produce similar results when configured similarly (@LI2021149).
- We aim to validate FIMS estimates against ADMB-based models (ASAP, BAM, SS3) for key quantities and assess FIMS's random-effects feature using Woods Hole Assessment Model (WHAM; @STOCK2021105967).

# Methods 

- An age-structured operating model (OM) with grouper-like life history was used to simulate data (@LI2021149).
- ASAP, BAM, SS3, WHAM, and FIMS were configured as estimation models (EMs). Configurations for WHAM and FIMS included the use of fixed versus random effects for recruitment deviations.
- The OM was simulated 100 times to generate replicate datasets.
- Model performance metrics included:
  - Median relative error (MRE) and median absolute relative error (MARE) for key quantities such as spawning biomass ($SB$), recruitment ($R$), and fishing mortality ($F$). 
    - Relative error (RE) was calculated as $\frac{\hat{\theta}_i}{\theta_i} - 1$, where $\theta_i$ is the true value for simulated dataset $i$ and $\hat{\theta}_i$ is the value estimated from fitting the model to the simulated data. 
    - 95% confidence intervals of the MRE were calculated using the binomial distribution to estimate bias of each EM for a given OM (@THOMPSON1936122).
    - To summarize the bias across simulations and years for each model, we calculated the median RE across years for each simulation and then took the mean of these medians across simulations (@STOCK2021105967).
    - MARE across all years and simulations was calculated to quantify the overall accuracy, providing a measure of the magnitude of estimation errors.
  - Convergence rate of EMs.

# Results

- Time series estimates from all EMs captured the overall trends observed in the operating model, and the variability across the 100 simulations was consistent among EMs and closely matched the variability of the OM (@fig-recruitment-time-series).

```{r}
#| label: fig-recruitment-time-series
#| fig-cap: "Time series of recruitment estimates from each estimation model (EM; ASAP, BAM, SS3, WHAM with fixed effects, WHAM with random effects, FIMS with fixed effects, and FIMS with random effects) compared to the true values from the operating model (OM). Colored lines represent the median recruitment across 100 simulation replicates for each EM, and colored shaded areas indicate the 95% confidence intervals. The black line in the center shows the true median recruitment from the OM, with the gray shaded area representing the OM's 95% confidence interval."
#| fig-alt: "Line plot showing recruitment over time for 7 estimation models (EM; ASAP, BAM, SS3, WHAM with fixed effects, WHAM with random effects, FIMS with fixed effects, and FIMS with random effects) compared to the operating model (OM). Colored lines represent the median recruitment estimates for each EM, and colored shaded areas show the EMs' 95% confidence intervals. A black line shows the true recruitment from the OM, and a gray shaded area indicates the OM's 95% confidence interval."
#| fig-width: 15
#| fig-height: 20
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: 100%
#| echo: false
knitr::include_graphics("../C0/figure/recruit_all_em_vs_om.png")
```

- Across all EMs, the MRE remained close to zero, indicating minimal bias in estimates relative to the OM. The distributions of REs showed consistent patterns among EMs over time (@fig-fishing-mortality-boxplot). In the early years, REs were generally small and narrowly distributed, reflecting high precision in model estimates. In later years, the distributions widen across all EMs, indicating increased variability in the estimates. Overall, both the central tendency and variability of REs were similar across EMs, with FIMS configurations performing comparably to the other EMs (@fig-spawning-biomass-violin).

```{r}
#| label: fig-fishing-mortality-boxplot
#| fig-cap: "Relative error (RE) in fishing mortality over time from each estimation model (EM). Each facet represents one year from 1 to 30, and within each facet, seven boxplots show REs across 100 simulation replicates for the EMs: ASAP, BAM, SS3, WHAM with fixed effects, WHAM with random effects, FIMS with fixed effects, and FIMS with random effects."
#| fig-alt: "Panel of 30 facets showing relative error (RE) in fishing mortality for years 1 to 30. Within each facet, seven boxplots represent the distribution of relative errors across 100 simulations for each EM: ASAP, BAM, SS3, WHAM with fixed effects, WHAM with random effects, FIMS with fixed effects, and FIMS with random effects. Boxplots show the median, interquartile range, and outliers, illustrating the bias and variability of each EM over time."
#| fig-width: 15
#| fig-height: 20
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: 100%
#| echo: false
knitr::include_graphics("../C0/figure/re_boxplot_f.png")
```

```{r}
#| label: fig-spawning-biomass-violin 
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of relative error (RE) in spawning biomass across all years and 100 simulation replicates for each estimation model (EM). Violin plots summarize the full distribution of REs for ASAP, BAM, SS3, WHAM with fixed effects, WHAM with random effects, FIMS with fixed effects, and FIMS with random effects."
#| fig-alt: "Violin plot showing the distribution of relative error (RE) in spawning biomass for seven estimation models: ASAP, BAM, SS3, WHAM with fixed effects, WHAM with random effects, FIMS with fixed effects, and FIMS with random effects. Each violin represents REs across all years and 100 simulations. The distributions are centered near zero and have similar shapes."
#| fig-width: 15
#| fig-height: 20
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: 100%
#| echo: false
knitr::include_graphics("../C0/figure/re_sb.png")
```

- Summary statistics of absolute relative error across all years and simulations showed similar performance among EMs for all quantities of interest (@tbl-mare). For fishing mortality, FIMS showed similar performance to the other models, with MARE values between 2.50 and 2.54. Recruitment estimates had the largest errors across all models, and FIMS results (MARE = 3.99) were comparable to those from ASAP, BAM, SS3, and WHAM (MARE = 3.96–4.00). For spawning biomass, FIMS produced among the lowest MARE values (2.01–2.08), indicating high estimation accuracy relative to the OM. Overall, the error metrics for FIMS were similar to those of the established models across all quantities, demonstrating that FIMS achieved comparable estimation performance.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-mare
#| tbl-cap: "Median absolute relative error (MARE; %) in fishing mortality, recruitment, and spawning biomass. Metrics include MARE with 95% confidence intervals (mare_lower and mare_upper), mean absolute relative error (mean_are), and the standard deviation of absolute relative error (sd_are) calculated across all years and 100 simulation replicates (n = 3000 observations per metric)."
#| tbl-alt: "Table summarizing estimation performance for seven estimation models: ASAP, BAM, SS3, WHAM with fixed effects, WHAM with random effects, FIMS with fixed effects, and FIMS with random effects. Metrics include MARE with 95% confidence intervals (mare_lower and mare_upper), mean absolute relative error (mean_are), and the standard deviation of absolute relative error (sd_are) calculated across all years and 100 simulation replicates (n = 3000 observations per metric)."
#| echo: false

# Read the CSV data 
mare_data <- read.csv("../C0/figure/median_absolute_relative_error_summary.csv")

# Display the data as a table
knitr::kable(mare_data, "pipe", caption = "Median Absolute Relative Error Summary") |>
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = F) |>
  kableExtra::column_spec(2, bold = T) |>
  kableExtra::landscape() |>
  kableExtra::row_spec(0, angle = 45) # Rotates header text
```

- Convergence rate of EMs (working in progress)

# Discussion

- FIMS produced estimates of spawning biomass, recruitment, and fishing mortality that were highly consistent with those from established stock assessment models, indicating comparable estimation accuracy across metrics of interest.
- Small differences among models likely resulted from implementation details such as recruitment deviation constraints, initialization of numbers-at-age, and recruitment bias correction (working in progress).
- Comparisons with WHAM to evaluate the random-effects implementation in FIMS are currently under investigation.
- These results support the use of FIMS as a reliable and flexible framework for next-generation fisheries stock assessments while maintaining consistency with existing modeling approaches.

# References {-}
